serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bakwan Jagung consists of approximately 3 fritters, weighing around 120 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Corn fritters, fried — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central corn fritter reference and FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable fritter data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bakwan Jagung in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for corn fritters (FDC ID 172429 and related entries) with FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable fritter composition data. Macros cross-checked against published Indonesian food composition tables (DKBM Indonesia). Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of corn-based fried snacks — analogous estimate based on corn fritter GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Bakwan Jagung. GI of 62 estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for corn fritters and fried corn-based snacks (GI range 55–72 reported for similar products). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 37.4) / 100 ≈ 23.3, based on 120 g serving containing approximately 37.4 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~384 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Bakwan Jagung to occasional consumption, as the sodium content from added salt and seasoning contributes meaningfully to daily sodium targets; a single serving provides approximately 17% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI ~62) and moderate glycemic load (~23 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Bakwan Jagung in small portions and pair it with low-GI foods or protein, as its medium GI and moderate glycemic load can cause a meaningful postprandial blood glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds total fat and saturated fat; moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Bakwan Jagung should be consumed sparingly due to its deep-fried preparation contributing to elevated total fat (~13.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g), alongside moderate sodium levels that may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bakwan Jagung is a popular Indonesian fried corn fritter made from sweet corn kernels mixed with wheat flour, egg, spring onion, shallot, garlic, salt, and pepper, then deep-fried in oil until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy while the interior remains soft and slightly chewy, with the natural sweetness of corn balanced by savoury aromatics. It is a widely enjoyed street food and snack across Indonesia and is also served in Indonesian restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, a 120 g serving provides approximately 318 kcal, 37 g carbohydrate, 7 g protein, and 16 g fat, with moderate sodium (~384 mg). The medium glycemic index (~62) and moderate glycemic load (~23) mean it can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The deep-frying method increases total and saturated fat content, which is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Bakwan Jagung contains gluten (wheat flour) and egg, making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or egg allergies. It is generally considered halal and vegetarian-friendly when prepared without meat-based additives.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Bakwan Jagung?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bakwan Jagung was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA SR Legacy corn fritter data and FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable fritter composition, cross-referenced with Indonesian DKBM food composition data. GI was estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using analogous corn fritter entries. Confidence is medium due to the estimation basis rather than a direct exact match.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy corn fritter entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable fritter data; no exact Bakwan Jagung entry was found in primary regional databases. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the reference sources for this food type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the reference sources for this food type. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database corn fritter data; a direct GI measurement for Bakwan Jagung has not been published. The cuisine field is set to Indonesian reflecting the dish's origin; the input record listed Singapore as cuisine due to the field survey data source context. The food is served at Dapur Penyet - Funan in Singapore, an Indonesian restaurant, consistent with Indonesian origin classification. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from typical palm/vegetable oil frying profiles. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on typical deep-frying conditions with refined vegetable oil.
